Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 290 
10.3 The 'Genome Mapping' Metaphor 
The deliberate deployment of the scientific metaphor 'Genome Mapping' in 
the title of this compilation deserves comment. The compilers/editors of this 
work (associated with the Videha literary-scholarly movement, as the context 
suggests) are consciously repositioning the Panjee tradition within a 
discourse of modern scientific precision and analytical rigor. Just as genomic 
DNA encodes the biological heritage of an individual, the Panjee encodes the 
social-genealogical heritage of a community. 
The metaphor is apt in several ways: both systems track descent across 
multiple generations, both use systematic notation to compress complex 
information, both serve regulatory functions (the genome regulates biological 
development; the Panjee regulates social reproduction), and both can be 
'read' by specialists to extract information invisible to the untrained eye. The 
metaphor is also productively ironic: genomics is a universal, caste-blind 
science, while the Panjee is a caste-specific social technology — the 
juxtaposition highlights both the system's sophistication and its historical 
embeddedness in caste society. 
Section XI: Conclusion 
The document Jeenoam Maeping (450 A.D. – 2009 A.D.) — Mithilak Panjee 
Prabandha, Volume II (Khanda 2, Upatip), pages 2. to 2., is a segment of one 
of the most extraordinary genealogical archives in the world. It belongs to the 
Panjee Prabandha tradition of the Maithil Brahmin community of Mithila — 
a system of professional genealogical management that has been maintained 
continuously for approximately seven centuries. 
As a document of anthropological and sociological significance, it encodes: 
Patrilineal genealogies spanning six to nine generations per entry, across 
dozens of villages in Bihar and Nepal. 
Marriage alliance networks linking families across a geographic area of 
hundreds of square kilometers. 
Titles, professions, and social ranks spanning from medieval Sanskrit 
scholarship (M.M.U., Vaiyaakarana, Naiyayik) through colonial-era 
aristocracy (Raja, Maharaja, Babu) to modern meritocracy (Ph.D., I.A.S., 
M.B.B.S.).

Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 291 
Demographic evidence of urbanization, migration, and occupational change 
over three centuries. 
*Indirect evidence of women's social role, movement, and function within a 
patrilineal kinship system.* 
Cross-references to a larger corpus of Panjee volumes that together constitute 
an unparalleled multi-century social database. 
For scholars of Indian history, Maithili linguistics and literature, Sanskrit 
philosophical traditions, caste sociology, genealogical science, and the 
anthropology of kinship — this document, and the larger tradition it 
represents, constitutes a resource of the highest importance. Its careful 
scholarly edition and analysis represents a critical contribution to the 
preservation and interpretation of one of South Asia's most complex and 
durable social institutions.

Chapter 14 
Pampilā Pañjī, Khaṇḍa 3 — Nāgarāma: Comprehensive 
Analytical Report (Volume I) 
Comprehensive Analysis: Nāgarāma Panji Prabandh (Khanda 3) Videha 
Parallel Literature Series 
Comprehensive Analytical Report 
Nāgarāma Khanda (Khanda 3) of the 
Pampilā Panji Prabandh (Genome Mapping 450 AD – 2009 AD) 
jīnoma maipiṃga (450 e.ḍī. sa~ 2009 e.ḍī.) – pampilā ñjī 
prabandha 
Prepared by: Gajendra Thakur, Founder-Editor, Videha 
(videha.co.in) 
Videha Parallel Literature Movement | A Counter-Canonical Analysis 
I. Document Identity and Nature of the Source 
The document under analysis is Khanda 3 (khaṇḍa 3), the Nāgarāma section, 
of the Pampilā Panji Prabandh — a monumental Maithil Brahmin 
genealogical register stretching from circa 450 AD to 2009 AD. The full title 
rendered in the document itself reads: 'jīnoma maipiṃga (4 e.ḍī. sa~ 2 e.ḍī.) – 
pampilāka ñjī prabandha', which the compilers have imaginatively translated 
as 'Genome Mapping', acknowledging the panji system's function as a 
biological and social record of descent lines across fifteen-plus centuries. 
The panji is not simply a genealogical tree. It is simultaneously a legal 
instrument (marriage eligibility is determined by consulting it), a sociological 
archive (recording village affiliations, professional titles, and marital 
alliances), a linguistic record (preserving old Maithili orthography and place-

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 293 
name forms), and a political document (reflecting the distribution of learned 
Brahmin families across the Mithila region and its diasporas extending to 
Bengal, Nepal, Purnea, Muzaffarpur, Munger-Banka, Saharsa, Madhubani, 
Darbhanga, and Champaran). This Khanda covers the 'Nāgarāma' territorial 
cluster — a web of villages in the Madhubani-Darbhanga belt — and 
contains 225 numbered blocks spread across pages 3. to 3. of the printed 
edition. 
1.1 The Panji System: An Institution 
The Pampilā Panji tradition is traced to Harisingh Deva, king of Mithila (fl. 
14th century), who commissioned a systematic registration of Maithil 
Brahmin families under five Panji offices, each responsible for a territorial 
cluster. The Nāgarāma Panji was one of these five, covering the central and 
northern Madhubani region. The register was maintained orally and in 
manuscript form, updated by hereditary Panji officers (panjikāra). The 
present printed edition represents a modern consolidation and 'genome 
mapping' of those records — cross-referenced, indexed by serial number, and 
extended to living individuals as of approximately 2005–2009. 
The social function is critical: no Maithil Brahmin marriage is considered 
legitimate without panji consultation. The register determines whether a 
proposed alliance would violate the rules of sapinda (common ancestor 
within seven generations) or sagotra (same clan/lineage). The document thus 
continues to have immediate legal-social force for the Maithil Brahmin 
community worldwide, including in diaspora settings. 
1.2 Layout Conventions and Reading Grammar 
The document follows a formulaic grammar that the reader must decode to 
extract meaning. Every block is structured around a chain of descent 
expressed through the following grammatical particles in Maithili: 
suta / sutā / suto | son/s / daughter/s / two sons — the subject of the main 
descent line 
dau (dauhitra) | grandson through a daughter = maternal uncle-nephew 
linkage, indicating the person's mother's natal family 
ddau (dauhitrīdauhitra) | two generations maternal; marks the second 
maternal cross-reference 
ṭha. (ṭhākura) | honorific prefix; denotes a male head of household of high 
standing

The reading direction is: Personal name → titles/aliases → village of bride's 
family → her father's name → her father's father's name → marker 'dau' → 
maternal cross-link village → maternal grandfather's name → 'ddau'. Each 
such unit describes one marriage alliance. Multiple 'aparā' sections describe 
polygamous or subsequent alliances. 
II. Territorial Geography of the Nāgarāma Cluster 
The Nāgarāma Khanda is named for the central hub village of Nāgarāma, 
which functions as the nodal reference point for this cluster's panji office. 
The territorial extent documented in this Khanda is vast, encompassing 
villages across at minimum five modern districts of Bihar and beyond. The 
following geography is decoded from the block headings: 
2.1 Core Villages (Primary Settlement Zones) 
The primary genealogical villages — those whose male lines are traced as 
the 'main' descent — include: Sandahpur (sandahapura), Shishpur (śiśapura), 
Katka (kaṭakā), Khāngud (khā~gur̤ a), Sakurī (sakurī), Sarisab (sarisaba), 
Bhad-gāma (bhar̤ agāma), Gaur Sodarpur (gaura sodarapura), Raiyan 
(raiyāma), Digaundh (digaundha), Mahishī (mahiṣī), Kanhaulī (kanhaulī), 
Suvai (sakurī pallī), Phūldāhā (phūladāhā), Jamdaulī (jamadaulī), Harisauli 
(hasaulī), Jagaur (jagaura), Khorrī (khorara), Rohand (rohār̤ a), Pāchadī 
(pacār̤ hī), Parahi (parahī), Haripur (haripura), Samaiyā (samayā), and Sukhait

(sukhaita). These are the villages whose Brahmin families' descent lines form 
the structural backbone of the Khanda. 
2.2 Alliance Villages (Cross-Reference Matrix) 
The marriage-alliance villages — cited in the 'dau/ddau' positions — 
constitute a far broader network. Recurring alliance villages mentioned 
across hundreds of blocks include: Karamahā (karamahā), Valiyās (valiyāsa), 
Sarisab (sarisaba), Pandua (paṇḍuā), Khauāl (khauāla), Pālī (pālī), Saurath 
(saurāṭha), Sodarpur (sodarapura), Bhauralī (bhaura-khaṇḍabalā), Dandapāṇi 
(daṇḍapāṇi), Darihara (dariharā), Ekahara (ekaharā), Pabauli (pabaulī), 
Māṇḍar (māṇḍara), Kusaut (gusauta), Jajiwāl (jajjivāla), Bhelauṃc 
(belau~ca), Narauṇ (narauna), Pahilwārā (pahalavārā), Kujaulī (kujaulī), 
Uchit (ucita), Bharatpurā, Gusaut, Saktihā (satalakhā), and dozens more. 
This network extends geographically beyond the core Madhubani district 
into: Darbhanga (villages like Valiyās, Pandua, Sodarpur), Sitamarhi 
(Narauṇ), Muzaffarpur (Bachha-kotā area, Machakanautā), Purniyā (Nāhar, 
Banaili, Rāṇiṭol), Saharsa-Supaul (Dātha, Berāhī), Champaran (Canapur, 
Pacadhī), and Nepal (Janakpur belt). The diaspora reach documented 
includes Bhagalpur, Munger, and even references to urban postings. 
2.3 Village Name Standardisation Issues 
A significant research challenge in this document is the variable spelling of 
village names. The same village appears under multiple orthographic forms: 
'valiyāsa / baliyāsa / valiyāma' are all the same Valiyās; 'bhelau~ca / 
belau~ca / velau~ca' are all Bhelauṃca. This reflects the organic, oral 
transmission of the panji tradition and the multiple hands involved in 
compilation.

of 
approximately 60–80 village name variants. 
III. Deep Structural Analysis of Selected Blocks 
The following section provides close analytical readings of specific 
numbered blocks. Each block is treated as a unit of genealogical, linguistic, 
sociological, and historical data. The blocks are selected to represent 
different types of lineage structures, title clusters, geographic zones, and 
temporal depths.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 296 
3.1 Blocks 1–4 (Pages 3.2–3.3): Sandahpur–Sishpur Cluster 
Block 1 — (nagarāma sandahapura sa~ sihavār̤ a, Page 3.2) 
This is the opening block of the Khanda and encodes one of the most 
structurally complex lineage chains, beginning from a reference point coded 
as (270/2), which is a cross-index to another Panji register. The genealogical 
chain opens: 
ṭha. ā~khī sutā lāla pra. guṇākara udana paraśmani hīrākā karamahā sa~ nanū 
sūta gaṃgeśa dau (270/5) paḍuā sa~ pā~ paśupati dau ṭha. lāla suto 
nagaradhārī abadha nabahārī kau hariama sa~ bhavakṛṣṇa sūta prānnātha dau 
dariharā sa~ śivadatta dau 
Decoding this opening chain reveals several layers simultaneously: 
NAME STRUCTURE: The first named individual is 'ṭha. ā~khī' (Thākur 
Āṃkhī) — 'Āṃkhī' is an informal/affectionate name (literally 'eyes'), 
extremely common in Mithila's folk naming tradition where body-part 
names, nature names, and auspicious syllable combinations are used. His 
children are listed: 'lāla pra. guṇākara' (Lāl, known as Guṇākar), 'udana', 
'paraśmani', and 'hīrā'. The 'pra.' abbreviation (prasiddha = popular name) 
indicates that 'lāla' was the formal panji name but 'guṇākara' was how he was 
actually known — a crucial distinction showing the gap between registration 
names and living identities. 
ALLIANCE STRUCTURE: 'karamahā sa~ nanū sūta gaṃgeśa dau' tells us 
that the daughters of Āṃkhī married into the Karamahā village, specifically 
into the family of Gaṃgeśa, son of Nanū. The name 'gaṃgeśa' (Gaṃgeśa) is 
significant — it is a Navya-Nyāya philosopher's name, specifically borne by 
Gaṃgeśa Upādhyāya of Navadvīpa (Darbhanga origin, fl. c. 1325 AD), the 
founder of the Tattvacintāmaṇi school. This raises the question of whether 
this entry records an early alliance with the philosopher's family or a 
namesake tradition honouring him. The cross-reference '(270/2)' suggests the 
full lineage pre-dates this section and can be found in another Khanda. 
DESCENT CONTINUITY: 'ṭha. lāla suto nagaradhārī abadha nabahārī kau' 
— Lāl (= Guṇākar) had two sons: Nagardhārī and Avadhnavahārī. The 
coupling 'suto...kau' (suton...kau) is the dual form, indicating exactly two 
male heirs. 'hariama sa~ bhavakṛṣṇa sūta prānnātha dau' — Nagardhārī's 
family sought a bride from Hariam village, from the family of Prānnātha, son 
of Bhavakṛṣṇa. This is then counter-linked: 'dariharā sa~ śivadatta dau' — the 
maternal counter-link goes to Darihara village, specifically Śivadatta.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 297 
What this complex four-village network (Sandahpur origin → Karamahā 
alliance → Hariam bride → Darihara maternal link) in a single opening 
paragraph reveals is the principle that Maithil Brahmin marriage alliances 
operated as a quadrilateral system: one does not simply note a marriage, but 
records the full four-node genealogical context that determines future 
marriage eligibility for all descendants. 
Block 2 — Second Branch from Sandahpur (Page 3.2) 
Block 2 continues the Sandahpur–Sishvad lineage but focuses on a different 
sub-branch beginning with 'ṭha. udana' (Udana), a brother of Āṃkhī's 
children. The critical entry reads: 
ṭha. harṣamani pra. naharā suto bācāka: karamahā sa~ citradhara suta ṛddhi 
dau dariharā sa~ binū dau ṭha. bācā sutā bālāmukunda rājā gānā chedīkā 
Here 'harṣamani pra. naharā' (Harṣamaṇi, known as Naharā) has one son 
Bācā. The name 'naharā' is an interesting informal name from the Maithili 
folk lexicon, and 'harṣamani' is a compound of 'Harṣa' (joy/delight) + 'maṇi' 
(gem) — a style of naming reflecting the 'gem of joy' sentiment. Bācā's four 
children are: Bālāmukunda, Rājā, Gānā, and Chedī — note that 'Rājā' is used 
as a personal name, not a title. This is characteristic of Mithila naming 
practice where royal epithets (Rājā, Rāṇā, Thākur) circulate as given names, 
especially in high-status Brahmin families. 
'ṭhe. bāla mukunda sutā paṇḍuā sa~ pā~ buddhinātha suta pā~ thāḍū dau 
karamahā sa~ śyāmanātha dau' — Bālāmukunda's family is identified 
through double alliance: bride from Pandua (family of Thādū, son of 
Buddhinātha, himself a 'pā~' = learned Brahmin) and maternal link to 
Karamahā (Śyāmanātha). The title 'pā~' on both 'pā~ buddhinātha' and 'pā~ 
thāḍū' indicates this was a hereditary scholarly family of the Pandua cluster. 
Block 5 — Sandahpur to Shishpur Lineage (Page 3.2–3.3) 
Block 5, headed 'sandahapura sa~ śiśapura (367/2)', is structurally rich 
because it shows the branching of a single ancestral line into multiple 
geographically dispersed sub-families over several generations. The entry 
opens: 
ṭha. bandhu suto madhurāpati narapati pālī sa~ nandana suta manahara dau 
khauāla sa~ vidyāpati dau ṭha. madhurāpati suto vācha śivānandano belau~ca 
sa~ lālā suta bālacandra dau karamahā sa~ vaṃśīdhara dau

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 298 
The name 'narapati' (Narapati = lord of men) alongside 'madhurāpati' 
(Madhurāpati = lord of sweetness/Mathura) in the same sibling set shows the 
poetic naming conventions of the period. 'ṭha. bācha sutā citradhara 
caṇḍīdatta phatura rāma lakṣmaṇa' — four sons with markedly different 
naming styles: Citradhara (pictorial/artistic name), Caṇḍīdatta (gift of 
goddess Caṇḍī), Phaturam (vernacular Maithili-Hindi compound), and 
Lakṣmaṇa (Rāmāyaṇa epic name). This mixture of Śākta names (Caṇḍīdatta), 
Vaiṣṇava-epic names (Lakṣmaṇa), and folk Maithili names (Phaturam) in 
one sibling set reflects the religious pluralism of 17th–18th century Mithila 
households. 
'sakarāḍhī sa~ devakṛṣṇa suta śyāmalāla dau hariama sa~ ramānātha dau' — 
The bride for Citradhara's line comes from Sakrādī village, family of 
Śyāmalāl son of Devakṛṣṇa, with maternal link at Hariam (Ramānātha). This 
is the first appearance in Khanda 3 of the Sakrādī cluster, which emerges as 
one of the most frequently cited alliance villages across the entire Nāgarāma 
Khanda. 
The most significant entry in Block 5 is the sub-branch 'muktinātha sutā 
vighneśa dineśa bhaveśa maheśā' — four sons of Muktinaātha all bearing 
names ending in '-eśa' (lord), a poetic symmetry in naming that marks a 
cultivated literary family. The subsequent generation: 'ṭha. vidhneśa sutā 
vaccā pra. umeśa pīlū nareśa pūranā' shows the next generation using more 
informal names alongside formal ones — 'pīlū' is an extremely 
informal/affectionate diminutive. 
3.2 Block 33 (Page 3.11): Khāngud to Padahiyā — A Three-
Generation Scientific Profile 
Block 33 is one of the most intellectually significant in the entire Khanda 
because it records a lineage containing a professional scientist (yaṃtrajña = 
mechanical/instrument engineer) alongside traditional scholarly designations. 
yaṃtrajñadayānātha sutā tārānanda candrānandau sakarāḍhī sa~ lānhā suta 
gokulānanda dau pālī sa~ pā~ curmana dau (tārānanda suto narendra 
amarendro 
The prefix 'yaṃtrajña' (Yantrajna = one who knows/works with 
machines/instruments) applied to Dayānātha is a remarkable intrusion of 
modern professional vocabulary into the ancient panji genre. The panji 
traditionally used titles like 'vaiyākaraṇa', 'jyotiṣī', 'mahopādhyāya'. The 
appearance of 'yaṃtrajña' signals that this individual was an engineer or

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 299 
technical professional in the colonial or early post-independence period. The 
panji officer preserved this designation as part of identity, just as it had 
preserved 'pāṭhaka', 'jyotiṣī', and 'vaidya' for generations. 
Dayānātha's two sons: Tārānanda and Candrānanda. The alliance for this 
generation: 'sakarāḍhī sa~ lānhā suta gokulānanda dau' — from Sakrādī, the 
family of Gokulānanda son of Lānhā. Tārānanda's two sons Narendra and 
Amarendra represent the generation born roughly in the 1930s–1950s. The 
subsequent entry reveals a further evolution: 
candrānanda suto sañjaya: mahiṣī budhavāla sa~...harivaṃśa dau sakurī 
valiyāsa...sa~...dau saṃjaya suto...cainapura (baluā)... 
Candrānanda's son Sañjaya (Sanjay) represents the generation of the 1960s–
70s. His marriage alliance extends to Chainpur (Balua) — a location in 
Saharsa/Supaul district — demonstrating how the Nāgarāma panji network 
by the late 20th century spanned an increasingly wide geographic radius. 
'jayanātha suto sadācārī arthaudyamī ramānanda sūryamanando' — this entry 
is extraordinary. The name 'arthaudyamī' (Arthaudyamī = economic 
entrepreneur/businessman) is used as a personal designation alongside the 
formal name 'ramānanda', indicating that by the time this person was being 
registered, the panji had extended its professional title vocabulary to include 
modern economic categories. This family shows the complete arc from 
'yaṃtrajña' (engineer-grandfather) to 'arthaudyamī' (businessman-son) across 
two generations. 
Further in Block 33: 'lalana pra. śyāmānanda suto varuṇa| ānahila hariama 
sa~ guṇānanda suta nāgeśvara dau śrīmān guṇānanda suto sadāvattī 
nāgeśvara (pra. a.) ḍā. pro. arthaśāstrī (lakṣmeśvaro)' — here 'ḍā. pro. 
arthaśāstrī' (Dr. Professor, Economist) is inserted as a professional 
designation before a formal name. The full title cluster on one individual — 
Dr., Professor, Economist (Artha-śāstrī) — followed by the personal name 
Lakṣmeśvara shows how the panji adapted to absorb modern academic 
credentials as identity-defining markers. This professor-economist's lineage 
goes back to the 'yaṃtrajña' engineer in the same block, giving us three 
professional generations visible within the genealogical record.

records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 300 
3.3 Block 122 (Page 3.35): Kanhaulī to Māṃgaraunī — The 
Justice, the IAS Officer, and the C.P.M. M.P. 
Political and Administrative Professions in the Panji 
Block 122 contains one of the most politically significant clusters in the 
entire Khanda. It is headed 'kanhaulī sa~ māṃgaraunī (62)' and contains an 
extraordinary concentration of high modern public offices recorded within a 
single genealogical sequence: 
dūtakhī pra. lūṭaneśvara sutā bhaiyā candrā pra. sadde jāge hīṃgū nagirajā 
rājendrā: pālīsa~ nityānanda suta bholānātha dau pabaulī sa~ nārāyaṇadatta 
ddau śītala sutā jasṭisa amaranātha kamalanātha pro: trilokanātha I.A.S. 
vindunāthā dariharā sa~ digambara suta vaiyā. śivaśaṃkara dau didhosa~ 
muktinātha ddau 
Śītala's four children are listed: Justice Amarnātha, Kamalnātha, Prof. 
Trilokanātha (I.A.S.), and Vindunātha. The designation cluster here is 
unprecedented: a High Court Justice ('jasṭisa'), a Professor who is also an 
IAS officer (the rare combination of academic and administrative civil 
service), all within one sibling set. 
The marriage-alliance entry for this generation reads 'dariharā sa~ digambara 
suta vaiyā. śivaśaṃkara dau didhosa~ muktinātha ddau' — the bride came 
from Darihara village, family of Vaidyākaraṇa (Sanskrit grammarian) 
Śivaśaṃkara son of Digambara. This pairing of a Justice/IAS family in the 
groom's line with a traditional 'vaiyākaraṇa' family in the bride's line 
illustrates the social dynamics of mid-20th century Maithil Brahmin marriage 
alliances: modern credentials were valued, but traditional scholarly lineages 
remained prestigious marriage targets. 
Earlier in the same block: 'jyotiramaṇa sutā paṇḍuā sa~ guṇānanda suta 
mejara kṛṣṇānanda dau karamahā sa~ narasiṃha ddau' — the maternal 
alliance cites 'mejara kṛṣṇānanda' (Major Krishnānanda), a military officer 
from Pandua village. Military rank as a genealogical identifier appearing in 
the panji marks the early 20th century period of Maithil participation in the 
British Indian Army. 
Still further in Block 122: 'vakīla śaṃkarānanda sutā ambikānanda 
vivekānanda śitalānanda mitrānandā: narauna sa~ śarmabhunātha suta phuccī 
pra.

Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 301 
Ambikānanda, Vivekānanda, Śītalānanda, Mitrānanda — all spiritually 
charged names, the selection of 'Vivekānanda' being particularly notable as it 
echoes the name of the great 19th-century Vedānta reformer, suggesting the 
family's intellectual-spiritual orientation in the nationalist period. 
The climactic entry in Block 122 comes near the end: 'kālikānanda suto 
kulānanda: lakhanaura jhuṃjhārapura karamahā sa~ devānanda suta 
khagendra dau... vakīla śaṃkarānanda suto tulānanda C.P.M. (M.P.) 
caturānandā:' — here 'C.P.M. (M.P.)' — Communist Party of India (Marxist) 
Member of Parliament — appears as a political designation in the panji. 
Tulānanda served as a CPI(M) Member of Parliament, and this fact is 
recorded in the genealogical register as a defining identity marker for future 
marriage consultation purposes, just as 'jyotiṣī' or 'mahopādhyāya' had been 
recorded for previous generations. This is arguably the most ideologically 
startling entry in the document: a communist parliamentarian's credentials 
enshrined in a Brahmin genealogical register that governs marriage eligibility 
through descent and purity calculations. 
3.4 Block 148 (Page 3.42): Raiyan to Rājahad̤ ā — The Allahabad 
University Connection 
The Most Concentrated Academic Cluster 
Block 148 is exceptional for its concentration of academic credentials in a 
single family unit. It documents the 'Jigdev' (jayadeva) family of the Raiyan 
Sodarpur village: 
ma.ma. jayadeva sutā (EX.V.C. ALLAHABAD UNIVERSITY) ma.ma. pā. 
ḍā. umeśa rameśo khauāla sa~ yadunātha suta janaka dau sodarapura sa~ 
hanumāna ddau 
The full credential string attached to Jayadeva is breathtaking: 'ma.ma.' 
(Mahāmahopādhyāya — the highest traditional Sanskrit title) + 'EX.V.C. 
ALLAHABAD UNIVERSITY' (former Vice-Chancellor of Allahabad 
University, one of India's premier institutions). The fusion of the medieval 
Sanskrit honorific 'mahāmahopādhyāya' with the modern academic 
administrative title 'Ex. Vice Chancellor' on a single individual embodies the 
Maithil Brahmin ideal of being simultaneously rooted in the Sanskritic 
tradition and achieving distinction in the modern Western-educated 
administrative framework.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 302 
His sons: 'ma.ma. pā. ḍā. umeśa' (Dr. Umesh, also a Mahāmahopādhyāya and 
'pā.' scholar) and 'rameśa'. Dr. Umesh's children: 'pro. jayakānta (Writer 
Hist.ry of Maithile Literature) pro. kṛṣṇakāṃta bhūgarbhaśāstrī vijayakānta 
prabhākānta pro. sudhākānta' — five children. 'Writer History of Maithile 
Literature' is recorded in parentheses in Roman script as a distinguishing 
attribute — indicating that Prf. Jayakānta had published a history of Maithili 
literature, perhaps one of the early systematic studies of the field. 
'bhūgarbhaśāstrī' (Bhūgarbhaśāstrī = geologist) appears as a professional 
designation for Vijayakānta — geology as a modern science entering the 
panji vocabulary. 
The marriage alliance for this generation: 'narauna sa~ taskara ke śava suta 
surendra dau dariharā sa~ lakṣmīkānta ddau' — the bride's family from 
Narauṇ is identified with 'taskara' (Taskara) as a personal name before 
'keśava'. 'taskara' literally means 'thief/smuggler' but functions here purely as 
a personal name — Maithili folk naming includes deliberately inauspicious 
or 'anti-name' conventions (just as parents sometimes named children 'Chor', 
'Bikhāri', or similar words as protective amulets against evil eye or early 
death). 
The geographical reach of this family is also significant: 'Dr. Jayakānta 
suta...Phandaha sân son Parmananda dau...Pandua sân pâṃ Shivnandin d'dau' 
— the marriage went to Phandaha and Pandua villages, extending the 
alliance network across three distinct territorial clusters of the Nāgarāma 
Panji zone. 
3.5 Block 183 (Page 3.54–3.55): Rohad to Bhavānīpur — A 
Political Dynasty 
The Chaudhary (cau.) Baccū Dynasty and Regional Political Power 
Block 183 presents what is arguably the most politically concentrated lineage 
in the entire Khanda. Beginning with 'cau. baccū' (Chaudhary Baccū), it 
documents five generations of a family in which regional political power is 
explicitly recorded as genealogical identity: 
cau. baccū sutā ānanda kiśora yugala kiśora navala kiśora nagaravara kiśora 
nandana kiśora paravana kiśorā: valiyāsa sa~ suphala suta rājā dau (09/17) 
karamahā sa~ śītalādatta ddau 
Chaudhary Baccū had six sons, all bearing names ending in '-kiśora' (Young 
Lord): Ānanda Kiśora, Yugala Kiśora, Navala Kiśora, Nagaravara Kiśora,

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 303 
Nandana Kiśora, and Paravana Kiśora. The repetition of the suffix creates a 
naming system within the family — a signature pattern that allows instant 
identification of siblings and establishes a strong family brand. The 'cau.' 
(Chaudhary) prefix indicates zamindari/landed gentry status. 
The son Dharmanarayaṇa's descendants are then catalogued with remarkable 
specificity: 'harinārāyaṇa suvendra nārāyaṇa mapendra nārāyaṇa śobhendra 
nārāyaṇa surendra nārāyaṇā' — five sons again using a suffix-repetition 
system with '-nārāyaṇa'. Then: 'surendra nārāyaṇa suto śailendra vijayendra 
mahendra...vuārā' — 'vuārā' is an informal/family nickname. The names 
'śailendra' (Śailendra), 'vijayendra' (Vijayendra), 'mahendra' (Mahendra) are 
all Indra-compound names, another systematic family naming convention. 
The entry 'harinārāyaṇa sutā suśīla indra vinoda aśoka manojā' shifts to 
entirely modern, colloquial names — Sushil, Vinod, Ashok, Manoj — 
marking a generational break in naming convention that correlates with post-
independence urbanisation. The Indra-Nārāyaṇa compounds give way to 
single-syllable Bollywood-era names. 
Block 184 continues this lineage and records: 'yugala kiśora suto upendra 
nārāyaṇa: valiyāsa sa~ raghuvīra suta gayādatta dau (B 57//5) sarisaba sa~ 
batahana ddau' — noting '(B 57//5)' as a cross-reference to another register 
entry. 'upendra nārāyaṇa sutā rāmamohana śyāmanareśa' — the progression 
from 'nārāyaṇa' compound names to simple 'rāmamohana' and the adjective-
style 'śyāmanareśa' (Śyāma-Naresh = dark lord, also evocative of Kṛṣṇa) 
shows the continuing evolution of naming conventions within the same 
bloodline. 
Block 185 then enters the explicitly political sphere: 'parabala kiśora suto 
devendra nārāyaṇa: hāṭī pālī sa~ yadurāī suta prayāgadatta dau khauāla sa~ 
ugranātha ddau' and then 'jagadīśa nārāyaṇa sutā...gusauta sa~ kumarasuta 
śrīpati dau jajjivāla sa~ bhaiu ddau'. The final entry in Block 185 records: 
'(389//8) pati suto manīka: alaya sa~ janādamana suta anātha dau (330/2) 
sodarapura sa~ lakṣamana ddau' — cross-references '(389/8)' and '(330/2)' 
point to entries in other Panji Khandas, indicating this dynasty's descent is 
documented in at least three separate territorial registers, signalling its pan-
Mithila importance.

records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 304 
IV. Evolution of Professional Titles: A Longitudinal Analysis 
One of the most historically valuable analytical layers of the Nāgarāma Panji 
is the sequential record of professional designations across generations. 
Unlike modern genealogical records that note only names and dates, the panji 
preserved occupational identity as an intrinsic component of a person's 
genealogical signature. The following analysis traces the arc from traditional 
to modern professional titles across the 225 blocks: 
4.1 Traditional Scholarly Titles (Pre-1800) 
pā~ (pā~cāṃgika) | Hereditary almanac-maker / astro-calendar specialist. 
Appears in Blocks 1, 5, 16, 26 etc. 
jyo. (jyotiṣī) | Hereditary jyotiṣī / astrologer. One of the most common titles. 
Blocks 5, 14, 22, 38, 65, 91 etc. 
vaiyākaraṇa (vaiyā.) | Sanskrit grammarian. Blocks 27, 28, 55, 66, 91, 109, 
117, 148, 196 etc. 
mahopādhyāya (maho.) | Senior teacher/scholar, higher than 'paṃḍit'. Blocks 
11, 38, 65, 91, 126, 136 etc. 
ma.ma. (mahāmahopādhyāya) | Highest traditional Sanskrit title. Blocks 38, 
51, 91, 112, 148, 190 etc. 
paurāṇika | Specialist in the Purāṇas; hereditary Kathā-performer. Blocks 66, 
106, 166 etc. 
nalatriśāstrācārya | Expert in three traditional sciences. Block 72, rare 
specialist title. 
tāṃtrika śiromaṇi | Crown of tantric practitioners. Block 196, very rare. 
4.2 Legal and Administrative Titles (Colonial Period, c. 1858–
1947) 
vakīla / Vakīl | Lawyer/Advocate. Blocks 48 (Brigadier Brajamohana), 66, 
122, 139 etc. 
mejara / Major | Military officer, British Indian Army. Block 120, 122 etc. 
ukīla harikānta | 'Ukīl' variant for Advocate. Block 1, very early use. 
dīvāna bhavānīdatta | Dīvān (revenue minister/administrator). Block 186. 
phaujadāra | Military/police officer. Block 97, early modern term. 
bhūgabhaṃśāstrī | Geologist. Block 148, post-independence professional 
science term.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 305 
4.3 Modern Professional and Academic Titles (Post-1947) 
yaṃtrajña | Engineer/Mechanical technician. Block 33. Earliest modern 
professional title. 
ḍā. / Dr. | Medical doctor or PhD. Blocks 33, 34, 35, 36, 100, 120, 148, 174, 
196, 222 etc. 
pro. / Prof. | University Professor. Blocks 120, 122, 148 etc. 
Ex.V.C. | Former Vice-Chancellor (Allahabad University). Block 148. 
Unique entry. 
arthaśāstrī | Economist (academic designation). Block 33. 
arthaudyamī | Economic entrepreneur/businessman. Block 33. Extraordinary 
entry. 
I.A.S. | Indian Administrative Service officer. Block 122. 
C.P.M. (M.P.) | Communist Party of India (Marxist) Member of Parliament. 
Block 122. 
jasṭisa | High Court Justice / Judge. Block 122. 
M.B.B.S. M.D. | Medical degrees recorded as genealogical identifiers. Block 
174. 
bhūgabhaṃśāstrī | Geologist (professional science). Block 148. 
Writer Hist.ry of Maithile Literature | Literary-historical authorship. Block 
148. 
L.L.B. | Law degree. Block 72. 
E.E. (abhiyaṃtā) | Executive Engineer (civil service). Block 62. 
bhūgarbha abhiyaṃtā-ravuni nideśaka | Mining Director/Engineer. Block 62. 
rabha.pū.pavahāra pava.sa.adhyakṣa | State Legislative Assembly Member. 
Block 174. 
ke.ndriya grāmīṇa rājya maṃtrī | Central Minister for Rural Affairs. Block 
174. 
The full arc from 'pā~' (almanac-specialist, pre-1400 AD) to 'C.P.M. (M.P.)' 
(Communist parliamentarian, post-1952) recorded within the same 
genealogical register, under the same formatting conventions, for the same 
functional purpose of marriage eligibility, is one of the most striking 
examples of institutional continuity and adaptive capacity in South Asian 
intellectual history.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 306 
V. Naming Conventions: A Linguistic and Cultural Analysis 
The Nāgarāma Panji is a treasure trove for onomastics — the study of 
naming practices. Across 225 blocks, thousands of personal names are 
recorded, allowing systematic analysis of naming conventions across roughly 
15 centuries and across different social strata within the Maithil Brahmin 
community. 
5.1 Compound Name Patterns 
The most common Maithil Brahmin naming patterns involve compounding 
with divine/cosmic elements. The following patterns are observed 
consistently: 
-nātha suffix | Lord, protector. E.g.: Gaṃgenātha, Śivanātha, Devnātha, 
Rāmnātha, Chandrannātha. Extremely common. 
-datta suffix | Gift of (a deity). E.g.: Śivadatta, Bhavānīdatta, Rāmdatta, 
Gaṃgādatta. Brahminic tradition. 
-ānanda suffix | Bliss of (a deity/concept). E.g.: Vivekānanda, Śītalānanda, 
Tārānanda. Post-19th c. increase. 
-kānta suffix | Dear to / beloved of. E.g.: Mithilākānta, Kāśīkānta, 
Kamalākānta. Aesthetic naming. 
-nārāyaṇa suffix | Vishnu-compound. E.g.: Śrīnārāyaṇa, Kṛṣṇanārāyaṇa. 
High Vaishnava influence. 
-kiśora suffix | Young-lord compound. E.g.: Yugala Kiśora, Ānanda Kiśora. 
Often in sibling sets. 
maṇi- 
/ 
-maṇi 
 
| 
 
Gem/jewel. 
E.g.: 
Harṣamaṇi, 
Candramaṇi, 
Bhuvaneśvarimaṇi. Both prefix and suffix. 
Informal/folk names | Āṃkhī (eyes), Naharā (canal), Pīlū (diminutive), 
Baucā, Ghonghī. Parallel naming system. 
Dual-name (formal + pra.) | Formal panji name + popular name. E.g.: 
'harṣamani pra. naharā', 'rāma pra. raghudeva'. Standard structure. 
Anti-names | Taskara (thief), Bikhārī (beggar), Garibnātha (lord of the poor). 
Protective folk convention. 
5.2 Temporal Shifts in Naming 
The panji reveals distinct chronological layers in naming conventions. In the 
early generations (inferred pre-1700 based on generation counting), Sanskrit 
Śaiva names predominate: Śivadatta, Śivanātha, Candraśekhara, Maheśvara,

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 307 
Nīlakaṇṭha. Moving into the 18th–19th centuries, Vaiṣṇava names increase 
sharply: Lakṣmīnārāyaṇa, Govarddhana, Kṛṣṇadeva, Rādhākānta. The Bhakti 
movement's influence on Mithila naming is traceable through the panji with 
greater precision than through any literary source. 
Post-1857, names reflecting colonial contact appear: 'Phaujadār' (military 
officer-name), 'Vakīl' as a given name rather than just a title prefix. Post-
1947, short, simple, modern names increase: Sanjay, Vijay, Arun, Manoj, 
Ashok, Sunil, Anita. By the 1980s–2000s entries, the naming patterns are 
virtually indistinguishable from general north Indian middle-class practice, 
reflecting acculturation to the national mainstream. 
5.3 Women's Names in the Panji 
Women are not recorded as individuals in the panji — they appear only as 
the axis of marriage alliances (village name + father's name = bride's 
identification). However, occasionally a woman's name surfaces in the text 
through the 'patnī anītā' (wife Anita) formula in Block 14 or similar 
parenthetical insertions. The near-total absence of women's names is itself a 
structural feature of the panji as a patrilineal instrument, reflecting the deeply 
asymmetric genealogical epistemology of the tradition. The Videha Parallel 
Literature Movement's critique of exactly this erasure is thus documented in 
the very source material this analysis examines. 
VI. Geographic Dispersal and Diaspora Patterns 
One of the most historically significant data-sets embedded in the Nāgarāma 
Panji is the record of geographic dispersal. The panji was designed to track 
family movement, since a man who moved to another region remained 
registered under his original village's panji but had to have his new location 
noted so that marriage eligibility could be correctly calculated across 
distance. 
6.1 Within-Bihar Dispersal 
The most common migration pattern visible in the text is movement from the 
Madhubani-Darbhanga core to Purniyā (Purnia) district, especially the 
Banaili estate area. Numerous blocks end with '...pūrṇiyā~' or '...banailī 
pūrṇiyā~', indicating that branches of core Nāgarāma families had settled in 
the Purnea region, likely attracted by the Banaili Raj's patronage of Maithil

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 308 
scholars and pandits. Block 16, 21, 29, and 39 all document such Purnea 
branches explicitly. 
Muzaffarpur district appears repeatedly, especially the Machakanautā 
(Machakanauta) and Viṣṇudattapur areas in Blocks 80–82. Bhagalpur-
Munger area appears in Block 71 (Teladīhā). Saharsa-Supaul areas appear in 
Blocks 64, 65 (Rāntī, Dīhā). Champaran (Camparān) appears in Blocks 217–
218. This indicates a systematic eastward and westward dispersal from the 
core Madhubani cluster. 
6.2 Nepal and Trans-Border Dispersal 
Several blocks document families settled in Nepal. Block 33 mentions 
'cainapura (baluā)' and the Madheshī belt locations. Block 35 references 
'navagar̤ hā naḍuāra karamahā' — 'Naḍuāra' and 'Navagaḍhā' are Nepal Tarai 
locations. Block 217 mentions 'Champarāndesh' (Champaran-Nepal border 
zone). The Nepal Tarai ('Madheshī') diaspora of Maithil Brahmins is thus 
documented in the panji alongside their Indian cousins, with marriage 
alliances crossing the border in both directions. 
6.3 Urban Professional Migration 
By the later entries (Blocks 120–225), a new migration pattern appears: 
families settling in named urban locations for professional reasons. Block 34 
mentions 'Śivanagara' (a town colony). Block 71 references 'capāhī gusauta' 
which is near Darbhanga city. Block 100 mentions 'cānapurā samīpe 
śivanagara'. Block 33 has 'khamagar̤ ā ratipār̤ a belau~ca sa~...' indicating 
settlement near specific urban nodes. By Blocks 174 and 183, entries 
reference 'rabha.pū.pavahāra' (state government posting), 'ke.ndriya...maṃtrī' 
(Central Government Minister) — indicating families that had fully entered 
the Delhi-national administrative sphere while retaining their panji 
registration in Madhubani. 
VII. The Cross-Reference System: Decoding the Numerical 
Codes 
The Nāgarāma Khanda deploys an extensive numerical cross-reference 
system that links it to other Khanda volumes of the same Panji Prabandh. 
These codes appear in parentheses throughout the text: '(270/2)', '(333)', '(B 
45//4)', '(A 19//8)', '(naga. 3.92//3)', 'na. (341/3)', 'ug. (17//3)' etc.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 309 
7.1 Types of Cross-References 
(270/2), (333)… | Bare numbers = cross-reference to another entry within 
the same Panji system by page/entry number 
(B 45//4) | B-prefix = cross-reference to a specific named sub-volume or 
Bayāna register 
(A 18//2) | A-prefix = cross-reference to another Panji Khanda (likely 
Khanda 1 or Khanda 2) 
(naga. / na.) | Abbreviation of 'Nāgarāma' = self-reference within this 
Khanda at given number 
(ug. / u.) | Possibly 'Ugana' or another territorial sub-cluster code 
(D. 82/5) | D-prefix = possibly 'Darbhanga' sub-register or a different panji 
office 
(J.E.) | Possibly 'Junior Engineer' — a modern professional credential code 
(śā. / ba.) | Possibly 'Śāka' era reference or Brahmin sub-group code 
The cross-reference system reveals that the Nāgarāma Panji is not a 
standalone document but part of a multi-volume, multi-office genealogical 
network. A family appearing in Block 14 of Nāgarāma Khanda 3 may have 
their deeper ancestral line recorded in entries (270/2) of another volume, 
requiring the panji officer to consult multiple registers before delivering a 
marriage eligibility ruling. This constitutes a genuinely complex information 
management system, maintained for centuries before the age of databases, 
through a combination of numbered manuscript folios and expert memory. 
IX. Significance Within the Videha Parallel History 
Framework 
The Videha Parallel Literature Movement, founded by Gajendra Thakur, 
explicitly positions itself as a counter-canonical alternative to Sahitya 
Akademi institutional authority. From this perspective, the Nāgarāma Panji 
Prabandh occupies a complex and somewhat paradoxical position: it is 
simultaneously an instrument of brahminic social control (governing 
marriage through patrilineal descent tracking, rendering women invisible as 
genealogical subjects, encoding caste hierarchy in every entry) AND an 
extraordinary repository of subaltern, folk, and vernacular data that 
institutional literary history has completely ignored. 
The panji records the 'vaidya' (doctor/physician) tradition alongside the 
'mahopādhyāya' tradition — local medical practice alongside high Sanskrit

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 310 
learning. It records the 'yantrajna' engineer, the 'C.P.M. M.P.', the folk names 
like 'Pīlū', 'Ghongī', 'Āṃkhī' that never appear in the canonical literary 
tradition. It preserves place-name forms ('Nāhar', 'Raiyan', 'Digaundh') in 
their living Maithili pronunciations rather than their Sanskritised or 
Persianised official forms. It documents the existence and location of dozens 
of villages that no other historical record preserves. 
The Videha framework's 'four-fold methodology' — Indian Rasa-Dhvani-
Vakrokti Aesthetics, Western Critical Theory, Navya-Nyāya Epistemology 
of Gaṃgeśa Upādhyāya, and the Videha Parallel History Framework — is 
directly applicable to this document. Through the Navya-Nyāya lens, the 
panji itself performs a kind of 'anumāna' (inference) logic: from the stated 
genealogical propositions (A is son of B, B married daughter of C from 
village X), one derives the inference (therefore A is eligible/ineligible to 
marry Y). The panji officer is functioning as a Naiyāyika logician applied to 
social epistemology. This connection between the panji tradition and the 
Navya-Nyāya tradition of Mithila — both arising from the same geographic, 
cultural, and intellectual milieu — deserves systematic scholarly attention. 
From the Western Critical Theory perspective, the panji's structural erasure 
of women's names and identities, its implicit hierarchy of gotras and śākhās, 
and its recording of 'purity' calculations as genealogical data all constitute 
what Gayatri Spivak might term the 'epistemic violence' embedded in 
archival practice. Yet the same document's organic absorption of communist 
parliamentarians, women scientists (fragmentary references), and modern 
professionals into its framework also demonstrates the system's remarkable 
adaptive resilience — not unlike the way the Brahminic tradition has 
historically absorbed heterodox elements into its own categories. 
X. Conclusions and Research Directions 
The Nāgarāma Khanda of the Pampilā Panji Prabandh is one of the most 
complex and data-rich genealogical documents in the Maithil literary 
tradition. Its 225 blocks, spanning an inferred timeline of roughly 600–700 
years of genealogical memory (with some reference points extending to the 
450 AD founding date), constitute an irreplaceable primary source for: 
53. DEMOGRAPHIC HISTORY — family sizes, marriage patterns, 
polygamy rates, migration patterns, mortality inference (through absent 
generations).

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 311 
54. PROFESSIONAL AND SOCIAL HISTORY — the evolution of 
occupational categories from Sanskritic scholarly titles through colonial 
administrative designations to modern academic and political credentials. 
55. ONOMASTICS — systematic naming convention analysis across 15+ 
generations and c. 600 years, revealing Śaiva-Vaiṣṇava religious shifts, 
Bhakti movement influence, colonial contact effects, and post-
independence cultural changes. 
56. HISTORICAL GEOGRAPHY — village network reconstruction, 
diaspora mapping, and identification of settlements not recorded in 
official land records or colonial gazetteers. 
57. LINGUISTIC HISTORY — preservation of archaic Maithili vocabulary, 
place-name forms, title systems, and grammatical structures that have 
been lost from the literary register. 
58. NAVYA-NYĀYA GENEALOGY — the Gaṃgeśa name appearing in 
Block 1 invites systematic investigation of the panji's connections to the 
Tattvacintāmaṇi philosophical tradition of Mithila. 
Critical editions of the panji, combined with the methodology of the Videha 
Parallel History Framework, would constitute a landmark contribution to 
Maithili studies, South Asian social history, and the history of knowledge-
management systems in pre-modern India. 
*This report was prepared as part of the ongoing Videha Parallel History of 
Mithila&Maithili Literature project. For the complete critical appreciation 
series covering 142 Maithili authors, see videha.co.in.*

Balirajpur, Rakhvari, Nagvad, Mahishi, Taruni, Naduar, Mazhaura, 
Gangaura, Dumra, Hariyam and dozens of sub-villages

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 313 
2. Nature and Structure of a Panjika 
A Panjika (pañjikā) is the foundational genealogical document of Maithili 
Brahmin society. It is not a mere family tree but a legal, ritual, and social 
instrument. Understanding its structure is essential before analysing 
individual entries. 
2.1 Structural Formula 
Every entry in this text follows a strict grammatical template derived from 
Sanskrit genealogical grammar. The basic unit is: 
[Name] suta / suto / suta: [son(s)] [village-A] sam [maternal-side-ancestor] 
sut [maternal lineage member] dau ... [village-B] sam [paternal-lineage-
side] ddau 
Key terms decoded: 
suta (suta) | sons (plural) 
suto (suto) | two sons (dual form) 
suta: (sutah) | one son (singular nominative) 
sa~ (sam) | from (village or gotra marker) 
dau (dau) | dau = dauhitra reference, the maternal side connection 
ddau (ddau) | double-dau, the dau's dau — grandmother's lineage 
pra. (pr.) | prakrit/common name (nickname or popular name) 
aparā (apara) | another wife / second branch 
kā (ka) / ko (ko) | feminine/neuter plural suffix for daughters 
jyo. (jyo.) | Jyotishi — astrologer-scholar designation 
vaiyā. (vaiya.) | Vaiyakarana — grammarian designation 
pāṭhaka (pathak) | hereditary scholarly title 
ma.ma. (m.m.) | Mahamahopadhyaya — highest scholarly title 
cau. (chau.) | Chaudhary — landed-gentry honorific 
ṭha. (th.) | Thakur — honorific for respected elder 
2.2 Village Codes 
Villages are referenced by shortened names throughout. They serve double 
duty: they identify residence and encode gotra-wise marital affiliation. The 
most frequently appearing villages in this khanda are:

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 314 
Sararasab, Jajival, Sodarpura, Pavauli, Pahadi, Dariharas, Khavaul, Mandar, 
Baliyas, Belaunca, Pandua, Satlakha, Ekahara, Sakrari, Kujaulli, Parhi, 
Gangauli, Pali, Naruna, Karamaha, Ghusauta, Ddigho, Tegval, Bherarai 
Each village name is a compressed reference to a known gotra-cluster. A 
Maithili reader does not need the full name; the shortened form instantly 
signals which kinship-group provided the bride or witnessed the dau 
relationship. 
3. Deep Analysis: Selected Blocks 
The following sections analyse individual entry-blocks in depth, unpacking 
genealogical, social, geographical, and onomastic information layer by layer. 
Block 226 (Page 3.94): Mangauni to Madarri — Devakinandan's 
Line 
devakīnandana dau (B 86//2) karamahā sa~ jālpādatta ddau|| yaṭā śaṃkara 
sutā ... rohār̤ a sakarār̤ hī sa~ mākiṇḍeya suta ṭhakkū dau ... jajivāla sa~ 
govinda ddau|| 
Opening citation — B 86//2:The notation 'B 86//2' is a cross-reference to 
another batch/volume entry. 'B' likely denotes a branch-volume or backup 
register. The double-slash number is a folio or sub-entry index. This 
intertextual referencing is characteristic of high-quality Panjika traditions: no 
information stands alone. 
Devakinandan — village Karamaha — dau Jalpadatta:The entry opens 
by establishing that Devakinandan's maternal connection (dau) comes from 
Karamaha, via a person named Jalpadatta. The 'd-d-au' (ddau) from Jajival 
via Govind then traces the dau's dau — two maternal generations are fixed at 
the outset, anchoring this entire branch within a confirmed kinship network. 
Yata Shankar's sons — Rohar Sakrari branch:Shankar's sons are marked 
by an unusual marker: '...' (ellipsis). In Panjika notation, ellipsis dots mean 
either the name is temporarily omitted (to be filled at verification) or the 
entry is pending confirmation. The dau for this sub-branch comes from 
Sakrari via Makandeya's son Thakku — giving us a Makandeya-Thakku 
patrilineal chain that is itself a datable reference point. 
Dayashankar's line — Sarisab branch:Dayashankar's sons marry into 
Sarisab via Bhairavadatta's son Bilat, with dau from Pali via Babu 
Shashidhar. The village Sarisab is one of the most prestigious villages in

Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 315 
Maithili Brahmin genealogy, so this connection elevates the lineage's 
prestige. 
Lutun and Ramanath — Ekahara branch:Lutun's sons include Ramanath 
(pr. Khatru — popular name Khatru) and another, through Ekahara via 
Sanaphul's son Man Khinu, with dau from Sakrari via Kanhai. The 'pr.' 
notation tells us Ramanath was known colloquially as Khatru — such dual-
naming is common when a child received an auspicious formal name but was 
called by an informal name at home. 
Ramanath's 
extended 
family 
— 
Sahityacharya 
Pracharya 
Dinanath:Among Ramanath's sons appears the title 'Sahityacharya 
Pracharya Dinanath' — a son who attained the Sahityacharya degree (a 
traditional Sanskrit literary doctorate) and became an Acharya (head 
teacher/principal). This is among the first glimpses of formal scholarly 
credentialing within this volume. His sons Bhaskar (engineer — abhiyaṃtā) 
and Pushkar represent a generational shift: the father is a classical scholar-
principal, while his sons have taken up modern professions. 
Bhagavanji's incomplete entry:'... Mangauni ... sam ... sut ... dau Phanda 
sam ... ddau' — three sets of dots indicate multiple pending verifications. 
This typically means the Panjika compiler was working from partial 
information, possibly from an oral source that had gaps. 
Block 226–227 (Page 3.94): Mangauni to Virasayar — The 
Vanshamani Sub-Branch 
śivana suto vaṃśamani gṛhimaji baliyāsa sa~ bhagīratha suta kuñjā dau ... 
viherār̤ hī sa~ maheramāna ddau|| 
Shivan's two sons — Vanshamani and Grihimani:The dual ending '-o' 
(suto) confirms exactly two sons. Vanshamani's maternal connection runs to 
Baliyas via Bhagirath's son Kunja, with the dau's dau through Viherari via 
Maheramana. This traces a four-generation maternal chain in one line. 
Vanshamani's son — Sarisab branch:'Sararasab sam Chhaban sut Bhikhiya 
dau' — Chhaban and Bhikhiya are father-son in the Sarisab cluster. Bhikhiya 
is a diminutive/popular name (probably for a person with a more formal 
Sanskrit name). The Darahara village provides the dau's dau via Brajanath. 
Brahmdatta and Dharmdatta — Jajival branch:Shobhanath's two sons 
receive their maternal connections through Jajival via Bhau's son Hemu. The 
village Pavanachobh (pavanacobha) appears for the dau's dau, providing

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 316 
Madhurapati — this rare village name appears repeatedly in this khanda, 
suggesting it was a significant kinship-node in the Nagrami sub-region. 
Brahmdatta's sons — Buvua pr. Gopal and Jagamohan:'Buvua pr. Gopal' 
— the prefix 'pr.' before Gopal tells us Buvua was the formal name but Gopal 
the popular one. This is an exact inversion of what we saw with 
Ramanath/Khatru above, showing both directions of formal/popular naming 
were common. 
Manniki's son Ruri — Satlakha branch:Mannik's son Ruri (Rurimji) 
draws his maternal side from Satlakha via Hari Narayan's son Lala, with dau 
from Pavoli via Aanhar. 'Aanhar' is another popular/informal name (it means 
'blind' in Maithili) used as a personal name, showing that names from 
physical characteristics were commonly given. 
Block 228 (Page 3.95): Mangauni — The Bhratrijan Line 
bhatrjana suto ānanda: pavaulī sa~ bhātū suta sītārāma dau ... valiyāsa sa~ 
bhaveśa ddau|| 
Bhatarjan and his line:Bhatarjan's single son Ananda draws his maternal 
side from Pavauli via Bhatu's son Sitaram, with ddau from Baliyas via 
Bhavesh. The choice of Sitaram (an avatar name) and the Bhatu-Sitaram 
father-son pair suggests this family's naming convention followed Vaishnava 
devotional practice. 
Vacha's son Shyamanath — Khandabala branch:Vacha's son is 
Shyamanath, connected maternally to Khandabala via Doni's son 
Dinabandhu. The ddau comes from Alay via Pathak Dubari. The title 'Pathak' 
for the ddau-side person indicates a scholarly family background — Pathaks 
were the highest Maithili sub-caste known for Vedic learning. 
Dayanath's sons — Adnanath and Premnath:Both sons' maternal chain 
traces back to Khavaul via Raghunath's son Durgadatt (with B-notation 
100//5), dau from Jajival via Umapatti. The 'B 100//5' cross-reference again 
shows the register is coordinated with a master volume. The 'apara suta' 
(aparā sutā) — sons of the other wife — are listed separately via Satlakha 
and Sodarpura, demonstrating that polygamous families had careful separate 
genealogical documentation. 
Kalanath's daughters — triple-dot entries:'Kalanath suta ... Darahara sam 
Rohini sut Shridatt ddau' with additional daughters via Pavoli via Pathak 
Raghuvir. When a daughter is listed, the entry tracks where she married out

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 317 
— into which village, via which family. Daughters are not omitted from the 
Panjika but their entries trace their destination rather than their descent. 
The nine sons of Apara (Sanphul line):An apara wife produces a 
remarkable 
nine 
sons: 
Hanumanadatt, 
Bhagavanadatt, 
Shankardatt, 
Chandidatt, Ishvaridatt — all five receiving their maternal chain via 
Belaunch via Jaynarayan's son Ray Lacha. This dense cluster of devotional 
names (Hanuman, Bhagavan, Shankar, Chandi, Ishvari) points to a Shaiva-
Shakta-Vaishnava eclectic naming pattern typical of 18th–19th century 
Mithila. 
Blocks 230–232 (Pages 3.95–3.96): Hati to Satlakha and 
Vilakhinad 
atha hāṭī pālī grāmārambha: bhavagaratha suto kālidāśa: māṇḍara sa~ 
rūcinātha suta gopa dau ... pālī sa~ viṣṇu ddau|| 
'Atha'— the formal commencement: The word 'atha' (atha) signals a new 
village or sub-section beginning. 'Atha Hati Pali gramrambha' — 'now begins 
the Hati Pali village section'. This liturgical opener (atha is the first word of 
Panini's grammar) reflects the sacred character of the Panjika — it is not 
merely a record but a ritual document. 
Bhavaratha to Kalidash — Mandar branch:Kalidash is the son of 
Bhavaratha, connected through Mandar via Ruchinathas son Gop, with ddau 
from Pali via Vishnu. 'Gop' (cowherd, a common name in Krishnabhakti 
families) and 'Vishnu' together signal a strong Vaishnava household at the 
dau/ddau level. 
Nath's three children — Vacha, Chhitari, Harikrishna:'Nath suta Bacha 
Chhitari Harikrishna' — all three from Sodarpura via Vadani's son Fakir, dau 
from Baliyas via Dharu. 'Fakir' as a personal name is significant: it is an 
Arabic/Persian-derived word meaning wandering ascetic, adopted as a 
personal name, indicating the cosmopolitan cultural mix in 18th-century 
Mithila where Sufi-Muslim vocabulary entered personal naming. 
Vacha's sons — Bhikhiya, Nanu, Baburaiya:A triplet from Sarisab via 
Purusottam's son Ratanu, dau from Karamaha via Mahinath. 'Bhikhiya' (from 
bhiksha, begging bowl — a mendicant-inspired name), 'Nanu' (child's word 
for small), 'Baburaiya' (compound of babu + raiya, a vernacular honorific) — 
three names that span the Sanskrit, rustic Maithili, and Urdu-influenced 
registers simultaneously.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 318 
The Yadvira sub-branch — Prayadatt, Hanumanadatt, Bhagavanadatt, 
Nauabati:Four sons of Yadvira (Block 231) connect to Belaunch via Umai's 
son Ruri, dau from Sakrari via Hemanath. 'Nauabati' is a compound name 
(nauba = drum, bati = lamp flame), an unusual poetic name. His son 
Yashhodhar connects through Vindhi Mahaura via Ramanath's son Ugranath 
— pointing to an inter-regional marriage link extending to Mahaura, a 
market town. 
Block 232 (Page 3.96): Hati to Vilakhinad — Chanurajha 
Haridash Jha 
dukhabhaṃjana suto dhana dullaho pavaulī sa~ gatirāma suta dukharana dau 
... valiyāsa sa~ paśupati ddau|| 
Name'Dukhabhanjan'— The Name as Prayer:'Dukhabhanjan' literally 
means 'destroyer of suffering' — an epithet of the divine applied as a 
personal name. This is a naming theology: the child is named after a divine 
attribute in the hope that the deity's grace attaches to the family. Many names 
in this text are similarly theophoric — they name the deity's function rather 
than the deity directly. 
Dhan and Dullaha — two sons:Dhan (wealth) and Dullaha (bridegroom) 
are popular/vernacular names rather than Sanskrit formal names. Their 
coexistence in the same entry with full Sanskrit dau-ddau chains shows that 
the Panjika accepts both high-register and low-register names without 
distinction — what matters is the kinship chain, not the name's etymology. 
Keshavnarayan's line — the Mandhan entry:Keshavnarayan's son 
Mandhan connects via Darahara via Gauridatt's son Khushihal, dau from Pali 
via Durgadatt. 'Khushihal' is a Persian/Urdu name (khush = happy, hal = 
state), again reflecting the cultural porosity of Mithila naming conventions. 
His son Shyam is described through Sakrari via Bhagirath's son Gangadatt — 
and then the genealogy continues into Budhval via Mahipati, creating a 
three-generation chain. 
Hemanath pr. Babujik — Baliyas branch:'Hemanath pr. Babujik' — 
Babujik is the popular name (a diminutive of Babuji, respectful address for a 
father/elder). The 'ji' suffix denotes respect. This naming pattern — taking an 
honorific used to address elders and converting it into a child's name — is a 
form of affectionate naming found across Mithila.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 319 
Harivamsh's son — Khavaul branch:Harivamsh's son traces back to 
Khavaul via Nehal's son Ramnath, dau from Pavanachobh via Rajamani. 
'Rajamani' (king-jewel) is a poetic compound name — it names the child as a 
jewel among kings. 
Block 238 (Page 3.97): Samauli Village — New Section Opening 
atha samauli nallī grāmārambha: pradyumna sutā rājīva locana kamala 
ghoghanā: jajivāla sa~ rāmadeva suta ghanī dau ... māṇḍara sa~ raghudeva 
ddau|| 
Pradyumna's four sons:The four sons — Rajiv Lochan, Kamal, Ghoghan 
— are named with a mix of Vaishnava (Pradyumna is Krishna's son; Rajiv 
Lochan means lotus-eyed — an epithet of Vishnu), botanical (Kamal = 
lotus), and rustic (Ghoghan = thunderstorm or drum) names. A single father 
naming one son with a sophisticated Sanskrit epithet, another with a simple 
Sanskrit noun, and a third with a Maithili vernacular word reflects how 
naming was not ideologically uniform even within a single household. 
Rajiv Lochan's son Hariharadatt:'Pavauli sam Pradyumna sut Bhikkh dau' 
— the son connects maternally back to Pavauli via Pradyumna's son Bhikkha 
(another mendicant-inspired name), dau from Pavanachobh via Purusottam. 
This creates a Pradyumna → Bhikkha → Hariharadatt naming chain that 
illustrates how name-reuse works across generations: 'Pradyumna' appears in 
both the grandfather's generation and the dau-side generation simultaneously. 
Hariharadatt's sons — Rajamani, Sonamani, Paramani:Three sons share 
the -mani (jewel) suffix, a common Maithili naming convention: rajamani 
(king-jewel), sonamani (gold-jewel), paramani (precious-jewel). Their 
maternal side runs to Sodarpura via Dharani's son Kamalnayan. 'Kamalnayan' 
(lotus-eyed) is the same epithet as 'Rajiv Lochan' one generation earlier — 
showing that divine epithets circulated continuously as names. 
'Thothri'branch — Kanhai pr. Vishnu-Mani: An 'apara Hariharadatt suto 
Thothrik' entry introduces a second-wife branch. Kanhai pr. Vishnumani tells 
us the child was named Kanhai (a name for Krishna, from the Sanskrit 
Kanha) but was formally known as Vishnumani. Two Vaishnava names for 
the same person: one the popular name used at home, the other the formal 
ceremonial name. Their maternal chain goes to Darahara via Garib's son 
Chanan — 'Garib' (poor, humble) being another Urdu-derived name.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 320 
Block 244 (Page 3.98): Samauli — Scholarly Titles and 
Occupational Names 
indrapati suta: śaṃkara: māṇḍara sa~ ... muktinātha dau ... sa~ ... ddau|| 
śaṃkara suta: nīlāmbara: 
Neelambar — Sky-Blue Cloud:Neelambar (nīlāmbara = blue sky/cloud) is 
a poetic name frequently given in Maithili families. It can reference Vishnu 
(who is blue-complexioned) or simply be a nature name. Neelambar's sons 
Mahipati and Upe appear — Mahipati (master of the earth = a king's epithet) 
and Upe (short for Upendra, another name for Indra or Vishnu). 
Upe's sons — Sanaphul, Phekan, Chhota:'Sanaphul' (flowering creeper), 
'Phekan' (one who throws/tosses — a colloquial Maithili verb-derived name), 
and 'Chhota' (small/little) represent three completely different naming 
registers in one family. Sanaphul is poetic-botanical; Phekan is colloquial-
verb; Chhota is the most casual possible name — often given to younger 
siblings. 
Sanaphul's son Govindadatta:Via Belaunch via Bhaiyaram's son Ankhi, 
dau from Pali via Panchai. 'Ankhi' means 'eyes' in Maithili — another body-
part name used as personal name. 'Panchai' is a corruption/popular form of 
Panchanan (five-faced Shiva). The chain Bhaiyaram → Ankhi → 
Govindadatta again mixes Sanskrit (Govindadatta = gift of Govind) with 
rustic Maithili (Ankhi). 
Phekan's sons Banmali and Krishnakant:Banmali (garland-wearer, an 
epithet of Vishnu) and Krishnakant (beloved of Krishna or one dear to 
Krishna) continue the Vaishnava naming pattern. Their maternal side is via 
Mandar via Devan's son Rudi — 'Rudi' is a shortened popular form of Rudra 
(Shiva), interesting in a Vaishnava-named family. 
Block 253 (Page 3.102): Balirajpur to Chopta — Navalkishor 
Mishra 
bavuā jī pra. jayakṛṣṇa sutā kumara jī amaranātha pitāmabara pra. a. 
navalakiśora jagadīśa satyanārāyaṇā: jajivāla sa~ bavujana suta bāvūlāla dau 
sakarār̤ hī sa~ lānhā ddau|| 
'Buvuaji pr. Jayakrishna'— layers of naming: This person has three names in 
one entry: Buvuaji (familiar/family address), pr. (popular name) Jayakrishna 
(victory of Krishna). The formal name Jayakrishna is the Panjika's canonical 
entry; Buvuaji is what the community called him; both are recorded.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 321 
Five sons — diversity of naming:Kumarji (prince, with the honorific 'ji'), 
Amarnath (immortal lord — Shaiva), Pitambar pr. A. Navalkishor (yellow-
robed — Vaishnava — plus a popular abbreviation to 'A.' suggesting an 
additional nickname now lost), Jagadish (lord of the world), Satyanarayan 
(truth-Narayan/Vishnu). These five sons span Shaiva (Amarnath), Vaishnava 
(Pitambar, Satyanarayan, Jagadish), regal (Kumarji), and philosophical 
(Navalkishor = new Youth) naming ideologies. 
Navalkishor's sons — modern professions:'Pro. Shyamanand, Injinivar 
Manoj Kumar, Subodh Kumar' — a professor, an engineer, and a third son. 
These occupational titles embedded in the genealogical record show the 
Panjika adapting to modernity: traditional Sanskrit names still appear, but 
their holders' occupations are now secular modern professions rather than the 
traditional Jyotishi, Vaiyakarana, or Pathak roles. 
Injinivar Manoj Kumar's sons — Sanjiv, Santosh, Raiyam:The maternal 
chain runs to Sodarpura via Ramesh's son Raghunandan pr. Hira. The 
popular name 'Hira' (diamond) for Raghunandan shows the continued use of 
gem-names as popular alternatives. 'Raiyam' in the son-list is unusual — it 
may be a place-name used as a personal name, or a variant of Rajaram. 
Block 254 (Page 3.103): Balirajpur to Chopta — Vishvambhar 
Mishra 'Vagish' 
*sāhityācārya prau. ḍā. viśvambhara miśra'vāgīśa'suto paṇḍuā sa~ pā~ 
śabhakhakhayā suta pā~ modānanda dau ... sararasaba sa~ mukunda ddau||* 
Sahityacharya Prof. Dr. Vishvambhar Mishra'Vagish':This entry is 
remarkable for its layering of titles: Sahityacharya (traditional classical 
doctorate in literature), Prof. (Professor — modern academic), Dr. (doctoral 
degree — modern university), followed by the nom de plume 'Vagish' (lord 
of speech — a pen-name). Four different credentialing systems are recorded 
in 
one 
genealogical 
entry: 
traditional 
Sanskrit 
learning, 
modern 
professorship, modern doctorate, and literary pseudonym. 
Vagish's two sons — Phandua branch:The maternal chain runs to Pandua 
via Pam Bhikhiya's son Pam Modananda, dau from Sarisab via Mukund. The 
'Pam' prefix (a contraction of Pandita — learned person) before both 
Bhikhiya and Modananda shows that even persons with humble popular 
names (Bhikhiya = beggar-related) could carry the 'Pam/Pandit' scholarly

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 322 
title, indicating achievement-based rather than purely birth-based scholarly 
recognition. 
Nilambar's sons — gazette reference:'Nilambar suta ... Gajhara Raiyam 
Sodarpura sam Manmohan sut: Mukund: Pandua sam Pam Garib sut 
Jagatmani dau ... Gangauli sam Nandi ddau' — 'Garib' (poor) as the name of 
a Pandita, and 'Jagatmani' (world-jewel) as his son, creates a striking 
contrast. The Gangauli-Nandi ddau link connects this family to one of the 
most respected Gangauli families. 
Rameshvar's sons — Mahadev, Bhairav, Chandranath:'Mahadev, 
Bhairav, Chandranath' — a Shaiva triplet. Bhairav's sons are described as 
'Kamendr Kumar, Timir Kumar, Virsayar' — 'Virsayar' here is a village 
name used as a personal name. The maternal line for Bhairav's sons runs to 
Mandar via Ugranath's son Harrikant, dau from Khandabala via Th. Avadhi. 
Block 294 (Page 3.116): Nagvad to Samauli — Tharanand's 
Scholarly Family 
ṭha. tārānanda sutā āśānanda virānanda jagatānanda vinodānanda: ... 
sodarapura sa~ puruṣottama suta jayanandana dau ... hararaama sa~ 
musahar̤ a ddau|| 1 III M.A. 
1934 III M.A. — a dateable credential:'III M.A. 1934' embedded in a 
genealogical entry is extraordinary: it records that Ashananda passed his 
M.A. examination (Third division) in 1934. This is one of the rare precise 
dates in the entire text, anchoring the genealogy to the modern calendar. It 
also reveals candidly that the M.A. was obtained in the third division — the 
Panjika records achievement without flattery. 
Virananda's sons — Double M.A. and engineers:'M.A. Double 
Yashodananda, Engineer Lalananda Engineer, Shyamananda Pleadser, 
Bunnulal I.T. Commission, Chhotu M.A.' — in a single family: two 
engineers, one double M.A., a lawyer (Pleader), a member of an Income Tax 
Commission, and another M.A. This is a mid-20th century professional 
family in full transition from classical to modern education. The Panjika 
meticulously records each credential. 
Virananda's Khavaul connection:'Vamshamani sut Lekhnanth dau 
Sodarpura sam Goshai ddau' — Lekhnanath (lord of writing) as a name in the 
maternal chain is fitting for a family producing M.A. holders. Goshai (a 
Shaiva ascetic title used as personal name) on the ddau side suggests the

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 323 
maternal-maternal family maintained classical religious traditions even as the 
primary line modernised. 
Acharyya Vinodananda:'Acharya Vinodananda' is listed with the title 
'Acharya' — an educational title. His son Ghurananda ('Ghuran' = one who 
wanders or turns — a rustic Maithili name) and Raju (another rustic informal 
name) show again how classical names and rustic popular names coexist 
within a single generation. 
Apucchha's sons — Suryananda, Shobbhananda, Jivananda:Three sons, 
all with the -ananda suffix: Suryananda (sun-bliss), Shobbhananda (beauty-
bliss), Jivananda (life-bliss). Their maternal connection is to Khandabala via 
Mukund Singh's son Devraj Singh — here 'Singh' appears as the kshatriya 
component, showing inter-varna marriage links are documented in the 
Panjika. 
Block 307 (Page 3.120): Nagvad to Rashad — The Vakil

'Vakil Brajmohan'— the embedded professional title: The title 'Vakil' 
(lawyer/advocate) precedes the name, treating it as part of the identity. This 
is the Panjika adapting to the colonial-era legal profession: the Bar 
qualification has become as heritable and identity-defining as the traditional 
Jyotishi or Vaiyakarana titles. 
The sons — a law family:M.S. Narendramohan (Master of Science — 
unusual for a legal family, suggesting an interdisciplinary career), 
Pranamahan,

Pracharya 
(Acharya-Principal),

father produces: a scientist, a principal, a law professor, multiple lawyers. 
The Sodarpura maternal connection:'Sodarpura sam Vasudeva sut 
Gangadhar dau ... Sakrari sam Keshav ddau' — the maternal side's 
Vasudeva-Gangadhar chain (both Vaishnava names) and the ddau-side 
Keshav (another Vishnu epithet) confirm that this legally modern family 
maintained Vaishnava naming conventions on both maternal lines.

Both appearing together in the dau-ddau pair signals maximum social 
prestige for this Pashupati line. 
Brahmpati's sons — Saheb and Bachhru:Via Kujaulli via Gaurinath's son 
Rami, dau from Mandar via Vabu. 'Rami' (a short form of Ramadeva or 
simply the feminine Rama) as a male name shows gender-fluid name usage. 
Saheb's sons run via Darahara via Abhinandan's son Keshav — Keshav is 
Vishnu's epithet (the beautiful-haired one), maintaining the Vaishnava 
thread. 
Narpati's son Rudrpati — Ekahara connection:Rudrpati (lord of Rudra = 
Shiva) connects via Ekahara via Vidudi's son Aniruddha, dau from Gangauli 
via Kriparam. Aniruddha is one of the four vyuhas of Vishnu in Pancharatra 
theology — here used as a personal name. The family naming theology 
deepens: Narpati, Rudrpati, Aniruddha span Vaishnava theology carefully. 
Madhurpati's two sons — Shripati and Dharapati:A further '-pati' pair: 
Shripati (lord of Sri/Lakshmi = Vishnu) and Dharapati (lord of the earth = a 
king's epithet). Via Sarisab via Nilambar's son Jivanath, dau from Baliyas via 
Ramdi. 'Ramdi' as a personal name (a popular shortened form of Ramadevi) 
for a male shows another instance of flexible gender-coding in Maithili 
names. 
4. Thematic and Cross-Cutting Analysis 
4.1 Name Typology Across the Khanda 
The names recorded in this khanda can be grouped into seven recurring 
typological categories: 
Category | Examples | Significance 
Sanskrit Theophoric | Hariharadatt, Govindadatta, Bhagavanadatt, 
Muktinath | Deity-gift or deity-lord compounds; most common type 
Sanskrit Epithet | Rajiv Lochan, Kamalnayan, Neelambar, Vanshamani | 
Poetic descriptive names; often Vishnu/Shiva epithets 
Maithili Vernacular | Phekan, Chhota, Ankhi, Ghoghan, Buvua | Informal, 
often body/nature/colloquial; used as popular names 
Persian/Urdu Loanwords | Fakir, Khushihal, Garib, Dullaha | Cultural 
porosity; Sufi/Indo-Persian naming layer 
Botanical/Nature | Sanaphul, Banmali, Kamal, Ghoghan | Flower, plant, 
storm names; poetic tradition
