records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 328 
Credential-Names | Sahityacharya, Vakil, Injinivar, I.P.S., I.A.S. | 
Professional titles embedded as name-prefix/identity 
Blessing/Aspiration | Chiranjeev, Dukhabhanjan, Amarnath, Ananda | 
Names expressing desired qualities for the child 
4.2 The Dual-Name System (Formal + Popular) 
A structurally consistent feature across every section is the dual-name 
notation using 'pr.' (pra. = prakrit/popular name). The Panjika records both 
names because the formal Sanskrit name governs ritual and legal contexts, 
while the popular name governs daily social life. Key observations: 
Direction 1 — Sanskrit formal, vernacular popular:Ramanath pr. Khatru; 
Hemanath pr. Babujik; Bhavtosha pr. Madhava. The Sanskrit name is the 
Panjika's authoritative entry. 
Direction 2 — Vernacular formal, Sanskrit popular:Buvuaji pr. 
Jayakrishna. Here the family title (Buvuaji) is the entry, and the formal 
Sanskrit name is the 'pr.' 
Direction 3 — One Sanskrit, one Persian:Dullaha pr. various entries — 
Urdu-derived name alongside Sanskrit. Shows neither tradition dominated 
exclusively. 
Implication:The dual-name system reveals that Maithili Brahmin families 
operated bilingually and bi-culturally in their naming. The Panjika's 
insistence on recording both names is a sociolinguistic document of code-
switching in premodern Mithila. 
4.3 Professional Credentials as Genealogical Data 
Across the khanda, the Panjika progressively expands its definition of 
'heritable credentials' from classical titles to modern ones: 
*Classical: Jyotishi (astrologer), Vaiyakarana (grammarian), Pathak 
(scholar), Mahamahopadhyaya (M.M.), Sahityacharya 
Transitional: Sahityacharya + Pracharya + Dr. (Vishvambhar Mishra Vagish, 
Block 254) 
Modern: M.A., M.Sc., Engineer, Pleader/Vakil, I.T. Commission, I.P.S. 
D.I.G., I.A.S.* 
The trajectory covers roughly 300 years of educational history. The earliest 
entries (Blocks 226–232) contain only Sanskrit titles; by Blocks 293–307, 
modern university degrees dominate; by Block 345–346, civil service ranks

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 329 
at I.P.S./I.A.S. level appear. This makes the Panjika an inadvertent history of 
Maithili Brahmin engagement with colonial and post-independence 
modernity. 
4.4 Gender Documentation 
Though the Panjika is overwhelmingly patrilineal, daughters appear in two 
contexts: 
As daughters (ka/ko suffix):When daughters are listed, their entry records 
their marital destination — which village and family they married into. This 
is the 'daughter-out' record. 
The I.A.S. daughter notation (Block 346):'Sutashch Prathama I.A.S. Putri' 
— 'and also daughters; the first I.A.S. among daughters.' This exceptional 
notation records a daughter's professional achievement, not her marital 
destination. It represents a profound shift in what the Panjika considers worth 
recording for women: from destination-in-marriage to achievement-in-career. 
The apara (second wife) system:Polygamous families carefully separate the 
genealogies of different wives' children. The 'apara suta' (another wife's sons) 
notation ensures legal genealogical clarity — which children belong to which 
mother matters for inheritance and ritual rights. Multiple 'apara' branches in a 
single entry (see Block 228 with nine apara sons) show the systematic rigour 
of this documentation. 
4.5 Geographic Network 
The village references in this khanda create a map of the Nagrami sub-
region's kinship geography. The most frequently referenced villages cluster 
into three functional categories: 
Prestige villages:Sarisab, Sodarpura, Jajival, Khandabala, Belaunch, 
Pandua. Appearing in dau/ddau position for high-status families. 
Bridge villages:Mandar, Baliyas, Darahara, Pavauli, Ekahara. Medium-
frequency, connecting multiple lineages. 
Peripheral villages:Pavanachobh, Viherari, Tegval, Naruna. Lower 
frequency, often in ddau (grandmother's side) position. 
The dau-ddau network means that every person is simultaneously a member 
of their own village's cluster AND connected to at least two other village-
clusters through their mother and maternal grandmother. This creates a web 
of mutual kinship obligation across the entire Nagrami region.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 330 
4.6 Chronological Markers 
Precise dates are rare in Panjika tradition, but this khanda contains several: 
B-notation cross-references:'B 86//2', 'B 100//5', 'B 62/3' etc. refer to other 
volume/folio entries. These suggest the Panjika was compiled in multiple 
stages, with cross-referencing between volumes. 
'1934 III M.A.'(Block 294): Places the Ashananda generation definitively in 
the 1930s. Working backward, his grandfather Tharananda would be c.1880–
1900; great-grandfather c.1855–1880. 
'Calcutta H.C. Jury'(Block 347): Places Patak Baun in the colonial period, 
likely late 19th century, given that his grandson-generation entries involve 
M.A. degrees and modern engineers. 
I.P.S./I.A.S. entries (Blocks 345–346):Post-1947 Independence entries, 
given that the I.P.S. and I.A.S. replaced the Indian Police Service/Indian 
Civil Service only after independence. 
These markers allow a rough chronological calibration: the earliest entries in 
this khanda (those with no modern titles) date to c.1650–1750 A.D.; the 
densest cluster of entries is c.1800–1900; the most recent entries reach the 
1980s–2000s. 
5. The Panjika as Literature and Social Document 
The Panjika is simultaneously several things: 
Legal instrument:It establishes gotra, lineage, and marital eligibility. 
Marriages in Maithili society were (and often still are) contracted only after 
Panjika verification to confirm absence of prohibited-degree relationships. 
Historical archive:The embedded professional titles, village networks, and 
name-patterns constitute an unintentional social history of Mithila across 
300+ years. 
Linguistic record:The names themselves are a corpus of Maithili, Sanskrit, 
Persian, and Urdu vocabulary as used in real-life naming contexts from the 
17th to 21st century. 
Theological document:The naming patterns reveal which deities were most 
invoked in naming, how Shaiva and Vaishnava traditions coexisted, and how 
devotional practice translated into domestic nomenclature.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 331 
Sociological data:The apara-wife documentation, the daughter-destination 
entries, the credential-recording, and the village-network encode the social 
structure of Maithili Brahmin society with a precision no ethnographic text of 
the period achieves. 
The 'Genome Mapping' framing of the title is apt: the Panjika traces the 
biological, social, and cultural DNA of a community across fifteen centuries 
— not as narrative history but as a dense, compressed, maximally 
information-rich genealogical code. 
6. Notes on the Videha Parallel History Framework 
In the context of Gajendra Thakur's Videha Parallel Literature Movement, 
this Panjika is significant as a counter-canonical source. The official Maithili 
literary canon recorded by Sahitya Akademi tends to highlight individual 
authors and their texts. The Panjika, by contrast, records the community as a 
whole — its farmers, scholars, lawyers, engineers, second wives, daughters, 
and blind men (Aanhar) alongside its Mahamahopadhyayas and I.A.S. 
officers. 
The Videha Parallel History framework, which emphasises subaltern voices 
and non-canonical texts, finds in the Panjika exactly such a non-canonical 
source: a document compiled not by literary institutions but by kulgurusara 
(family genealogists) for entirely practical social purposes, yet containing a 
richer social history than most formally composed chronicles. 
The Panjika's inclusion of occupational markers for women (Block 346's 
I.A.S. daughter), Urdu loanwords in personal names, mendicant-inspired 
names (Fakir, Bhikhiya), and village-level naming customs (body-part names 
like Ankhi; verb-names like Phekan) all constitute the 'what the canon left 
out' material that the Parallel History framework seeks to recover.

Chapter 16 
Pañjī Prabandha, Khaṇḍa 3 (Nagramā): Blocks 359–466 
(Volume I) 
Pañjī Prabandha — Khaṇḍa 3 (Nagramā) | Analytical Report 
Genome Mapping (450 A.D. – 2009 A.D.) 
pamapilāka ñjī prabandha — khaṇḍa 3 (nagarāma) 
A Comprehensive Analytical Report on the Maithil Brahmin Genealogical 
Register 
Blocks 359–466 | Pages 3.156–3. 
1. The Nature and Structure of the Panjī Text 
The forty pages under analysis constitute a portion of the third volume 
(Khaṇḍa 3) of the Panjī Prabandha — literally the 'administration of 
genealogical records' — governing the Maithil Brahmin community of the 
Mithila region (present-day northern Bihar and the Tarai belt of Nepal). The 
text is titled 'Genome Mapping (450 A.D. to 2009 A.D.),' an evocative 
modern metaphor applied to what is, in essence, a biological and social 
lineage database extending over fifteen centuries. 
The Panjī system is one of the most elaborate indigenous documentary 
traditions in South Asia. Originally established under the Karnāṭa dynasty 
king Harisingh Deva (c. 1300 A.D.) and institutionalized by the Panjī 
Prabandha, the register tracks patrilineal descent (gotra), village affiliation 
(mūla grāma), and cognatic/agamic relationships across thousands of 
families. It has been maintained by hereditary Panjī scholars (Panjīkāra or 
Panjīvāla) who certify marriages to prevent prohibited cross-cousin and

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 333 
close-kin unions. No Maithil Brahmin marriage was traditionally recognized 
without verification in the register. 
This volume is organized in numbered blocks (here running from 359 to 
466), each devoted to a cluster of families typically identified by a sacred 
address (the invocation 'oṃ [deity]-nāthāya namaḥ') and one or more 
ancestral village names. The geographic trail winds through Darbhanga, 
Madhubani, Sitamarhi, Muzaffarpur, Supaul, West Champaran, Purnia, and 
across into Nepal (Simraon, Goṭham, Vindhī, etc.). The coverage is thus a 
social cartography of Maithil Brahmin settlement from the medieval period 
to the digital age. 
2. Structural Components of Each Block 
Each numbered block is a micro-chapter with a consistent internal 
architecture. Understanding this architecture is prerequisite to any analytical 
reading. 
2.1 The Invocation Header 
Every block opens with a Sanskrit invocation in the form 'oṃ [divine 
name]āya namaḥ' or 'oṃ namaḥ [deity]'. This is not merely decorative. The 
deity named correlates with the primary gotra or kula-devatā (clan deity) of 
the lineage group being recorded. Examples across these blocks include:

Vicāḍhī (Hulāsh)

Rāṃṭī

Pilakhvāḍ

records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 334 
The progression of deity names — overwhelmingly Śaiva (Rāmeśvara, 
Nīlagrīva, Vṛṣabhavāhana, Śūlapāṇī, Mahākāla, Bhīmaśaṃkara, Āśutoṣa) 
with rare Vaiṣṇava or goddess references — reflects the historically Śaiva 
orientation of Maithil Brahmin religious culture, particularly strong in the 
Darbhanga Raj heartland. 
2.2 The Village Declaration (grāmārambha) 
After the invocation, a new village is often introduced with the phrase 'atha 
[village name] grāmārambhaḥ' (herewith begins the [village name] section). 
Some blocks compress multiple villages. The density of village citations — 
Kuā, Rāṃṭī, Mallichhām, Parahaṭ, Māṇḍar, Khauāl, Darbhara, Saurath, 
Naraun, Pabauśal, Sarisab, Pahalacom, Satalakhā, Belauñca, Jijāl, Karamahā, 
etc. — creates a topographic matrix of Maithil settlement across the Mithilā 
plain. 
2.3 The Genealogical Entry Formula 
The core entry formula is: [Person A] suta/sutā/suto [Person B] :: [village 
name] sa~ [grandfather's name] suta [father's name] dau [mother's village] 
sa~ [maternal grandfather] ddau. This encodes: 
Symbol / Abbreviation | Meaning in Genealogical Context 
suta / sutā / suto | Son(s) / Daughter(s) of the preceding person 
[village] sa~ ... dau | 'dau' = dauhitra sambandha — the mother's natal 
lineage (father's side) 
[village] sa~ ... ddau | 'ddau' = dvidauhitra — the mother's mother's lineage 
(maternal grandmother's side) 
pra. | Abbreviation of prakāśita or prasiddha — 'known as' (a popular alias) 
vaiyā. | Short for vaiyākaraṇa — a grammarian; a scholar title 
ma.ma. | Short for mahāmahopādhyāya — the highest traditional scholarly 
title 
jyo. | Short for jyotiṣī — an astrologer 
ṭha. | Short for ṭhākura — a landed gentry title 
cau. | Short for caudharī — a community headman 
pā~ | Short for pā~r̤ e / pāṇḍe — a specific sub-designation of Brahmin 
(B XX/YY) | Cross-reference to another block and line in the register 
The dual-maternal tracking (dau and ddau) is particularly significant: it 
records two generations of maternal descent simultaneously, allowing the

Panjī scholar to verify that no bride and groom share a common ancestor 
within the prohibited degrees on BOTH the paternal AND maternal sides. 
This is genealogical computing of remarkable sophistication, operating 
without modern databases but through a highly structured text protocol. 
3. Deep Analysis: Selected Blocks 
3.1 Blocks 359–362: The Vicāḍhī–Kuā Cluster (nagarāma / 
Vicāḍhī / Kuā) 
Block 359 opens with the Ananta sub-lineage (anantasuto baidyanātha 
dāmodara — 'sons of Ananta: Baidyanātha, Dāmodara') in the Māṇḍar gotra-
cluster. The immediate descendants of Baidyanātha disperse through the 
villages of Darbhara, Sarisab, and Sodarpur, reflecting the typical pattern of 
scattered settlement across a radius of 20-40 km. The maternal affiliations 
(dau/ddau) connect to Satalakhā, Bhelaunc, Pāli, and Naraun — all core 
Maithil Brahmin settlement nodes. 
The Dāmodara branch (dāmodara suto lakṣmana dinabandhuko — 
'Dāmodara's sons: Lakṣmaṇa and Dinabandhu') is a classical bifurcation. 
Lakṣmaṇa's line (lakṣmana suto khagapati hariko — 'Lakṣmaṇa's sons: 
Khagapati, Hari') extends to Khagapati whose four sons (meghanātha, 
devanātha, hṛdayanātha, premanātha) are recorded with their full maternal 
pedigrees. This is a five-generation chain visible in a single paragraph-block. 
Critically, Hṛdayanātha's son Yadunātha (hṛdayanātha suta yadunātha) is 
connected to Darbharā through the Meśa-Minā lineage — a specific inter-
village alliance that the Panjī tracks. Yadunātha's son Bhāyalāla connects to 
Vaśiloyā village through Sonī sub-clan (sonī suta īśvaradatta dau). This chain 
— Ananta → Baidyanātha → Dāmodara → Lakṣmaṇa → Khagapati → 
Hṛdayanātha → Yadunātha → Bhāyalāla — represents eight generations, and 
if we accept the ca. 450 A.D. start-date for the register's scope, this particular 
branch was likely established in the 17th–18th century given typical 
genealogical depth. 
Block 360 (vāsukīnāthāya namaḥ, Kuā to Rāṃṭī) opens with the Kuā village 
section (atha kuā tisau~ta grāmārambha:). The Jaganātha → Bhaiyo/Girdhari 
bifurcation is illustrative: Bhaiyo (bhaiyo) generates Kamalanayam and 
Apachhi through the Sōdapur (sodarapura) maternal connection, while 
Girdhari (giradhārī) generates Cirañjīva and Devakṛṣṇa. Cirañjīva's five sons

Block 362 (oṃ somanāthāya namaḥ) records the Haridev → Sadīānanda 
branch: Sadī's sons (Bācā, Thothā, Nenā) are spread across different maternal 
networks. Bācā's sons (Bhīmadatta, Kāśīnātha) generate further bifurcations, 
and the cross-reference '(B 41//8)' in Block 359 signals a deliberate link to an 
earlier entry — the register maintains internal consistency through these 
cross-references, functioning like hyperlinks in the manuscript tradition. 
3.2 Blocks 363–367: The Parahaṭ–Cāṇapurā Cluster (parahaṭa / 
cāṇapurā) 
Block 364 is remarkable for the presence of the Mahāmahopādhyāya title 
(abbreviated ma.ma.) — the highest traditional scholarly honour in Maithil 
culture — associated with the Śrī Khagesh and his descendant Rājā 
Vidyānidhī Hṛdayanidhi. The full title chain ma.ma. khageśa suto ma.ma. 
rājā pra. vidyāni hṛdayanidhi signals that both father and son held this 
distinction, which was extremely rare and indicates a family of exceptional 
Sanskrit learning. 
The Rājā's sons include Catuṣkraṇḍa-Yajñakartā Dhīrendra (cātuścaraṇa 
yajñakartā ma.ma. dhīrendra), described as a four-Vedic sacrificer. This is 
one of only a handful of references in these forty pages to actual ritual 
performance — most of the register is purely genealogical — making it a 
rare documentary trace of lived Sanskrit learning within the genealogical 
framework. 
Block 365 (Cāṇapurā, Parihārī, Badri-Ṭhākura) records the Raghunātha →

bifurcation. 
The

Lakṣmaṇa, Chatrakara are each based in different villages (Sōdapur, Pāli, 
Darbharā), and Ānkhī's sons (Saṃsādhara, Rājā, Manamohana, Fatarī) 
demonstrate four simultaneous settlements in Belaunc, Pāli, Pabauśal, and 
Hariama — the classic Maithil pattern of 'dispersed but connected' agnatic 
clusters. 
Block 366 (oṃ mahākālāya namaḥ, Cāṇapurā) records the Lakṣmaṇa → 
Chakauḍī branch. Chakauḍī's four sons (yadunātha, durgānātha, kīrtinātha,

3.3 Blocks 370–376: The Mangaurī–Khōjapura Scholarly Cluster 
Blocks 370–376 document arguably the most intellectually distinguished 
family cluster in the entire forty-page corpus: the descendants of Sadu-
Upādhyāya Rāmabhadra in Mangaurī (maṃgaraunī). 
Block 370 opens with the extraordinary Rāmabhadra pedigree: his sons 
include Paṇḍitarāja Padāhita (paṇḍitarāja padāhita), Mahāmahopādhyāya 
Madhusūdana (ma.ma. madhusūdana), Mahāmahopādhyāya Baidyanātha-
Pitāmbara 
(ma.ma. 
baidyanātha 
pra. 
pitāmbara), 
Sadu-Upādhyāya 
Umananda, Mahāmahopādhyāya Bāgīśa, Mahāmahopādhyāya Śatridaṇḍī-
Sannyāsī. The sheer density of scholarly titles in a single sibship is 
unparalleled in these pages and marks the family as the great intellectual 
dynasty of the Darbhanga region in the medieval and early modern period. 
The Rāmabhadra Dynasty — Scholarly Titles Recorded Paṇḍitarāja (King of 
Scholars) │ Mahāmahopādhyāya (5× in siblings) │ Sadu-Upādhyāya │ 
Sannyāsī-Scholar │ Śatridaṇḍī (holder of the triple staff of renunciation) 
M.M. Madhusūdana's sons include M.M. Gośāṁī Madana and Baladeva. 
Gośāṁī Madana's son Gañgādatta, and his son Śivadatta, extend the 
scholarly line. The Baladeva branch produces Gośāṁī Jīvanātha, Sonāī (alias 
Rāmanātha), and Hariātha — the latter a name connecting the family to the 
Vaiṣṇava tradition. 
Block 373 (oṃ rāmeśvarāya namaḥ, Mangaurī) records M.M. Pitāmbara's 
sons: M.M. Śatrilocana, M.M. Daṇḍapāṇi (alias Dhanañjaya), M.M. 
Ṭīkākāraka Ṣaḍyantri Gokulātha. Gokulātha's title Ṣaḍyantri (holder of the 
six-fold [scholastic] system) indicates a pandit who mastered all six 
Vedāṅgas. His sons M.M. Sarvsvadātā Raghunātha and Lakṣmīnātha 
continue the lineage, and Raghunātha's son Jayakṛṣṇa is recorded in Block 
373. 
The grandchildren of M.M. Bhavānīdatta (kavi śaṃkaradatta, nanu pra. 
rudradatta, pā~khī pra. iśvarīdatta, ramādatta, mānāpura govindadatta, 
phaturī pra. tīrthadatta) represent the diaspora of this great family across

śaṃkaradatta' — 'the poet Śaṃkara-datta' — is a direct record that this person 
was known as a poet, extremely rare autobiographical data embedded in a 
genealogical list. 
Block 374–376 track the further dispersal: Nanu Rudradatta's son Mannulāl 
and Sonelāl; Sonelāl's son Lālā → Rāmapāla → Śivajī (alias Śivanandana); 
and eventually into the 20th century with Manojakumāra (B.Sc. Hons.), 
Babhlā alias Sañjīvakumāra (M.A.), Ajaya alias Cunnā (B.Tech Mechanical 
Engineering). These modern academic qualifications, inserted into the 
genealogical stream, mark the point where the register transitions from 
traditional to modern credentialization. 
3.4 Blocks 379–382: The Pilakhvāḍ–Mangaurī Branch and M.M. 
Vāgīśa's Lineage 
Block 379 records M.M. Upādhyāya Umananda's sons Dharmeśvara and 
Guṇīśvara (dhameśvara, guṇīśvara). Guṇīśvara's son Gośāṁī Dharānātha 
generates a triad: Khagānātha, Bhajata, and Jhāuṃṭī. The Khagānātha branch 
is connected to Sōdapur through the 'Umananda → Bhavana' link. The 
'Jhāuṃṭī' line (jhāĕṭī sutā bhaiyana bavuā śaktinātha) leads to Bhaiyan's son 
Śrīnātha, and ultimately to 'Bhūla sutas — Joyanta and Subanta' — names 
that are characteristically Bengali-influenced, suggesting cross-cultural 
contact in the northern Champaran region. 
Block 382 (oṃ dukhaharanāthāya namaḥ, Mangaurī) records M.M. Vāgīśa's 
sons: Lāl alias Girdhara and Mahīdhara. The title Chātuś-caraṇa-Yajñakartā 
for M.M. Dhīrendra (son of Lāl/Girdhara) recurs here from Block 364, 
confirming a family tradition of Vedic sacrifice. M.M. Ratiśa's son 
Iśānanātha, and Iśānanātha's sons Śobhanātha and Rūpanātha, carry the 
scholarly tradition into what appears to be the 17th–18th century. 
3.5 Blocks 383–387: The Alaya–Lagamā Geographic Corridor 
Block 383 (oṃ śītikaṇṭhāya namaḥ, Rājagrāma) opens with 'atha alaya 
grāmārambhaḥ' — establishing Alaya as a new ancestral village hub. Vaṃśī's 
sons (Purañdara, Ratīśa, Caturbhūja, Mahī) are each connected to Belauñca 
through the Bāchani-Vīra maternal chain. 
The Candrapati branch (Block 383) is noteworthy for its sheer fecundity: 
seven sons (Devīdatta, Śītalādatta, Nārāyaṇadatta, Viṣṇudatta, Gaurīdatta,

Lakṣmīdatta, Paraśī) — all with the characteristic -datta suffix (gift of a god), 
a naming convention associated with devotional Śaivism. Each of these 
seven generates further branches. The Dīnabandhu sub-line (Dīnabandhu → 
Hariśchandra, Lalīcandra, Caṇḍeśvara, Sūryadeva, Amarendra) shows the 
transition to 19th century naming fashions: Hariśchandra (the famous noble 
king of tradition), Lalīcandra (a Romantic-era sensibility), Caṇḍeśvara 
(classical Śaiva), Amarendra (sovereignty-themed). 
Block 384 (oṃ siṃheśvaranāthāya namaḥ, Raśāḍha) records the Lāl → 
Nenā/Iśvarīdatta/Caṇḍīdatta/Girijādatta quadruple branching. The Nenā sub-
line connects to Darbharā through the Gaṃgeśa-Dharmadatta link. The 
Ratīśvara → Pītāmbara/Mandhara bifurcation leads to Māṇḍar through the 
Vaṭāī-Rāmdhara-Dharaṇī maternal chain — a particularly deep triple-
generational tracking. 
Block 385–386 (Pilakhvāḍ, Baligaḍha, Lagamā) introduce the Pāṭhaka sub-
community: Pāṭhaka Dubari (pāṭhaka dubari), Pāṭhaka Kṛṣṇapati, Pāṭhaka 
Ratīśa (rājamaṃgala pra. vucana), Pāṭhaka Satīśa, Pāṭhaka Girriśa, Pāṭhaka 
Ratiśa. The 'Pāṭhaka' prefix (literally 'reader/reciter') marks a sub-group of 
Brahmins whose traditional profession was recitation — a distinction 
preserved in the Panjī even as the families diversified economically. Pāṭhaka 
Gañgādhara's sons (Dharaṇīdhara, Ratīśa, Girriśa, Satīśa) and their further 
descendants extend across Darbharā, Ekaharā, Jijāl, and Karamahā. 
3.6 Blocks 388–395: The Baiganī–Baṃśī Rāja Dynasty 
Blocks 388–395 contain the most politically significant genealogy in the 
entire forty-page corpus: the Rāja lineage of Baiganī village, the aristocratic 
branch connected to the Banaili Rāmgaḍha royal house. 
Block 388 opens with Caudharī Devānanda, whose sons include 
Paramānanda, Parṇāma Hajārī, and Mahinakānanda. The key figure is Vābu 
(lord/master) Harirāla Siṃha, whose son Vābu Kalitanātha Siṃha becomes 
the progenitor of a ruling line. Kalitanātha's son Vābu Tīrthānanda Siṃha is 
noted. This line terminates — via Līlānanda Siṃha Bahādura's branch (Block 
388) — in the titles Rājā, Kumāra, and eventually reaches figures identified 
as Rājā Kalyāṇānanda Siṃha, Rājā Kīrtyānanda Siṃha Bahādura, and the 
extensive Śyāmananda Siṃha family (described as 'Saṃgīta Śāstra Mārtaṇḍa' 
— Sun of the Science of Music, a prestigious title).
